title: Poulet à la crème
subtitle: Normandy cream chicken braised in cider and cream
excerpt: Chicken thighs browned and then braised in dry cider and chicken stock, finished with a generous pour of double cream and a scatter of fresh tarragon. A dish from the apple-and-dairy heartland of Normandy that takes under an hour.
sourceNotes: Poulet à la crème is the central chicken dish of Normandy cooking, exploiting the region's two great products: cider and cream. It appears throughout French regional cookbooks and is a close relative of poulet Vallée d'Auge, which includes calvados. This version uses cider only, making it approachable without specialist spirits.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The braise can be made to the point before adding cream, then refrigerated for 24 hours and finished with cream on reheating.

content.text: This is Normandy in a pan: cider where the wine would go, cream where leaner regions would use stock alone, and apples in the background if you want them. The cream goes in at the end; add it too early and it breaks under sustained heat. Stir it in off the boil, return to a gentle simmer for five minutes, and it will thicken smoothly around the chicken without splitting.

Bone-in skin-on thighs carry more flavour than boneless and stay moist through the braise. If you use chicken breasts, reduce the covered braise to 15 minutes and watch them closely — they dry out fast past 72°C.

Season the {{chicken-thigh}} thighs generously on both sides. Heat the olive oil and {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a wide sauté pan over medium-high heat. Lay the thighs skin-side down and cook without moving for 6 to 8 minutes until the skin is deep golden. Turn and cook the other side for 3 minutes. Remove and set aside.

Pour off excess fat, leaving a thin film. Add the {{shallot}} and {{garlic}} and soften over medium heat for 3 minutes. Pour in the {{cider-dry}} of dry cider, let it bubble for 2 minutes, and scrape up the browned bits from the base. Add the {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock and {{thyme}} of thyme. Return the chicken skin-side up, cover, and cook over low heat for 20 minutes.

Remove the chicken. Remove the thyme. Stir the {{double-cream}} of cream into the pan liquid and simmer uncovered for 5 minutes until slightly thickened. Return the chicken, scatter the {{tarragon}} leaves over, and serve straight from the pan.

items.symptom: Cream has split into a greasy film
items.cause: Added to a boiling pan, or simmered too hard
items.fix: Remove from heat, let cool for 30 seconds, whisk firmly; use double cream rather than single as it is more heat-stable

items.symptom: Cider flavour is harsh and vinegary
items.cause: Cider not reduced enough before adding stock
items.fix: Let the cider simmer for at least 2 minutes before adding stock; most of the sharpness cooks off quickly

items.cause: Too much liquid for the pan size
items.fix: Remove the chicken and reduce the sauce uncovered over medium heat until it coats a spoon

items.symptom: Chicken skin has gone soft under the lid
items.cause: Skin submerged in the braising liquid
items.fix: Keep the thighs skin-side up so the skin sits above the liquid level; remove the lid for the last 5 minutes to re-crisp

content.text: Normandy sits in the north-west of France: Atlantic coast, apple orchards, dairy farms. The cooking reflects what the land produces. Cider replaces the wine of Burgundy; cream replaces the olive oil of Provence. Poulet à la crème, poulet Vallée d'Auge, and their variants are all iterations of the same logic — chicken braised in something acidic (cider or calvados) and finished with something rich (cream). It is domestic cooking from an agricultural region that happened to produce world-class dairy and fruit. Serve with boiled new potatoes or steamed rice.
